---
title: "Applied Economics Analysis"
subtitle: "Final Project Presentation"
author: "Montserrat Marti"
date: today
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: seahorse
    fonttheme: structurebold
    aspectratio: 169
    toc: false
    slide-level: 2
    header-includes: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage{caption}
        \captionsetup{font=small}
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
        \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
        \setbeamerfont{subtitle}{size=\normalsize}
---

---

## Motivation

\begin{itemize}
  \item Higher income improves mental health.
  \vspace{0.3cm}
  \item The relationship runs in \textbf{both directions:} Generates persistent effects on each other.
  \vspace{0.3cm}
  \item Mental health measured years earlier predicts well-being more strongly than current income.
\end{itemize}

## This Paper

\begin{alertblock}{Core Question}
Does a \textbf{lifetime of high earnings} translate into better psychological well-being and life satisfaction \textbf{in later life}?
\end{alertblock}

\vspace{0.4cm}
- I use **50+ years of longitudinal data** to trace individuals from early career to old age.
- I connect \textbf{lifetime labor income trajectories} to \textbf{psychological distress} and \textbf{life satisfaction} at age 50+.

---

## Data: Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID)

- **Longest running longitudinal household survey** in the world.
- Annual waves 1968–1996, biennial from 1997.
- Tracks individuals and their descendants over time.
- **85,536 individuals**.

\vspace{0.3cm}

**Main Variables:**

| Concept | Variable | Available Since |
|---|---|---|
| Hourly wage (real USD 2019) | `wage_real` | 1968 |
| Employment status | `empl_status` | 1979 |
| Psychological distress | K6 scale (0–24) | 1997 |
| Life satisfaction | 1–5 scale | 2007 |
| Health status | 1–5 scale | 1994 |

---

## Variable Construction

\textbf{Income trajectory:}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Historical average real wage: mean hourly wage across all employed person-years (CPI-U, base 2019).
  \item Income quintiles: Q1 (lowest) to Q5 (highest), requires $\geq$ 3 years of observed wage.
  \item Employment indicator: equals 1 if individual reports working in that year, 0 otherwise.
  \item Coefficient of variation (CV): standard deviation divided by mean of employment status across all observed years (age 20--65).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}

\textbf{Well-being outcomes (age 50+):}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{K6 distress}: Kessler-6 scale (0--24); scores $\geq$ 13 indicate clinically significant distress; available from 1997
  \item \textbf{Life satisfaction}: 1 = completely satisfied, 5 = not at all satisfied; available from 2007
\end{itemize}

---

## Real Wage Lifecycle by Income Quintile

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, height=0.7\textheight, keepaspectratio]{output/plot1_wage_lifecycle.png}
\end{figure}

\small \textit{Mean real hourly wage by 2-year age bins. Active employment observations only.}

---

## Employment Rate Over the Lifecycle by Income Quintile

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, height=0.7\textheight, keepaspectratio]{output/plot2_employment_lifecycle.png}
\end{figure}

\small \textit{Share employed by 2-year age bins. PSID 1979–2021.}

---

## Labor Trajectory and Well-being at Age 50+

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\input{output/table1b_summary.tex}
}
\small \textit{CV = coefficient of variation of employment status (lower = more stable). K6 and life satisfaction measured at age 50+.}

---

## Identification Strategy

\textbf{Two baseline specifications, each estimated for K6 and life satisfaction:}

\vspace{0.2cm}

\textbf{(1) Earnings trajectory:}
$$y_{it} = \alpha + \beta \cdot \bar{w}_i + \gamma X_{it} + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{it}$$

\textbf{(2) Employment stability:}
$$y_{it} = \alpha + \beta \cdot CV_i + \gamma X_{it} + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{it}$$

where $X_{it}$ includes age, current employment status, and health status; $\lambda_t$ are year fixed effects.

\vspace{0.2cm}

\begin{alertblock}{Threats to identification}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Reverse causality:} poor mental health reduces earnings and employment stability
  \item \textbf{Selection:} healthier individuals may sort into high-earning careers from the start
\end{itemize}
\end{alertblock}

---

## Preliminary Results

\centering
\scalebox{0.8}{
\input{output/table_regression.tex}
}
\small \textit{Controls include age, employment status and health.}

---

## Conclusion

\begin{itemize}
  \item High-earning individuals experience \textbf{lower psychological distress} 
  throughout their working lives but this gap \textbf{narrows significantly after 50.}
  \item Those with higher lifetime earnings report \textbf{greater life satisfaction} 
  in later life.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}

\begin{alertblock}{Next Steps}
Establish whether these patterns reflect a \textbf{causal effect} of lifetime 
earnings or \textbf{selection} using individual fixed effects and, if possible, 
an IV or event-study approach around retirement.
\end{alertblock}

---

## Thank You

\vspace{1cm}

\centering

\Large Questions?
